title: Trust and Distrust in ML: Using Algorithmic Properties of the Ground Truth Part 2
speaker: Shafi Goldwasser
affiliation: Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing/UC Berkeley/MIT/Weizmann Institute of Science

proof_stages.label: Cryptographic framing and robustness taxonomy
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames trustworthy ML using the cryptographic template of polynomial-time worst-case adversaries, explicit security definitions, and assumptions, then distinguishes distribution shift, adversarial examples, and planted backdoors as robustness problems.

proof_stages.label: Planted-backdoor threat model
proof_stages.summary: In the outsourced-training setting, a malicious trainer may return a Trojan model together with a secret activation key that maps any input to a nearby misclassified input, while the client is asked to distinguish benign and backdoored behavior only through feasible interaction.

proof_stages.label: Digital-signature black-box attack and impossibility of certification
proof_stages.summary: Assuming digital signatures, an easy checksum-style trigger is replaced by a signature verifier so that backdoors are undetectable and nonreplicable, and therefore any post-hoc robustness verifier would yield a cryptographic distinguisher.

proof_stages.label: White-box backdoors through corrupted randomness
proof_stages.summary: For a random Fourier features architecture, continuous-LWE-distributed weights can be computationally indistinguishable from honest Gaussian randomness while encoding a secret perturbation that flips the classifier, showing that even full model inspection need not reveal the backdoor.

proof_stages.label: Post-processing rather than detection
proof_stages.summary: Because detecting or certifying absence of backdoors can be impossible, the proposed response is to sanitize the model after receiving it; naive extra gradient descent and standard randomized smoothing are insufficient for the desired backdoor guarantees.

proof_stages.label: Random self-reducibility as mitigation principle
proof_stages.summary: The mitigation strategy uses random self-reducibility: reduce a target input to random instances drawn from a distribution where the model is known to be mostly correct, then recombine those answers to recover the target value.

proof_stages.label: Structured-ground-truth mitigation theorems
proof_stages.summary: For ground truths with well-behaved Fourier/analytic spectrum one can perform offline black-box cleanup, while for linear and real multivariate polynomial ground truths one obtains online mitigation via line-based and correlated-sampling reductions.

proof_stages.label: Trainer-side verification and verifiable data science
proof_stages.summary: For properties that cannot simply be mitigated after the fact, the talk points toward forcing the trainer to prove faithful training, while explaining why modern ML training strains classical verifiable-computing methods.

examples.name: Stickered stop sign for a self-driving car
examples.note: A stop-sign perturbation illustrates the familiar adversarial-example setting and contrasts attack-specific defenses with the talk's worst-case adversarial model.
examples.engagement_reason: the stickered stop sign is used as a famous motivating attack scenario, with no analysis beyond saying it could be misclassified as a yield sign

passes.reason: Ordinary shifted image and handwriting distributions are used to show that train-time assumptions can fail at inference.

the COVID-image and handwriting shifts are ordinary shifted data distributions used to show that training-distribution accuracy may fail under deployment shift

the COVID-image and digit-writing shifts are natural enemy cases showing an iid-trained classifier may fail under changed distributions

examples.note: The panda-with-noise, rotations, and hot-dog-like image examples illustrate that visually negligible or natural-looking changes can dramatically alter a classifier's output.

examples.name: Backdoored bank-loan classifier
examples.note: The loan-approval scenario makes the planted-backdoor notion concrete: someone with the secret key can slightly modify a rejected application so that the model accepts it.
examples.engagement_reason: the bank-loan classifier is traced through a concrete backdoor scenario: normal accept/reject behavior, a secret key, a slight loan modification, and a flipped rejection-to-acceptance outcome

passes.reason: An otherwise standard classifier with a hidden trigger is an attack showing robustness and trust can fail.

examples.name: Unsafe prompts and filters for LLMs
examples.note: LLM safety questions illustrate how adversarial examples and backdoor-like behavior need not be binary label flips but can mean eliciting a response the system is supposed to withhold.
examples.engagement_reason: unsafe LLM prompts and filters are invoked as an analogy for adversarial examples and filtering, without a specific prompt or filter being worked through

passes.reason: Unsafe prompts are discussed as adversarial inputs that defeat intended LLM safety filters.

unsafe prompts and filter-bypassing inputs are adversarial LLM cases meant to elicit forbidden behavior

examples.name: RSA devices reusing a prime
examples.note: The anecdote about many RSA moduli sharing the same prime illustrates that bad or controlled randomness can silently destroy security, paralleling the white-box backdoor attack through randomness.
examples.engagement_reason: the RSA-device example is concretely analyzed by noting shared-prime moduli and applying a GCD calculation to recover the repeated prime

passes.reason: RSA keys sharing a prime collapse the intended randomness and show key-generation security can fail.
shared-prime RSA moduli are collapsed low-entropy key instances that defeat security by a GCD attack
RSA moduli sharing a prime are a degenerate key-generation case that breaks security by a GCD attack.
RSA keys sharing a prime are a collapsed-randomness weak-key case, used to show that bad randomness can break security
RSA devices sharing a prime form a collapsed-randomness key ensemble with nontrivial gcds, used to show trusted randomness can fail.
the shared RSA prime collapses the intended independent key randomness and is cited as a GCD-based security failure
RSA moduli sharing a prime are used as a randomness-failure vulnerability, and the repeated prime is a collapsed/degenerate key-generation case.
RSA keys sharing a prime demonstrate a real cryptographic failure, and the repeated prime is a collapsed-randomness degeneracy
RSA devices reusing a prime demonstrate a security failure, and the shared-prime ensemble is degenerate because intended independent randomness collapses
Used as a security failure of RSA randomness, and the shared-prime batch is degenerate because independent key generation has collapsed to a common factor.
Used to show RSA security can fail when randomness collapses to a reused prime, making the key batch degenerate.
RSA keys sharing a prime form a degenerate common-factor key set and show bad randomness breaking security.

examples.name: Buggy calculator for addition
examples.note: A calculator that is usually correct on random inputs illustrates the random self-reduction idea of replacing a target computation by many randomized computations and taking the mode.
examples.engagement_reason: the buggy calculator is used to demonstrate a correction method: randomize an addition by adding and subtracting random r values repeatedly and taking the mode
